## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the right-leg drive circuit in a biomedical instrumentation system, and why is it preferred over direct patient grounding?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The right-leg drive (RLD) circuit is an active feedback loop used in bio-signal amplifiers (especially ECG) to maintain the patient's body at a stable common-mode voltage near the analog ground. The common-mode voltage is sensed by the INA, inverted and amplified by an auxiliary op-amp, and fed back to the patient's right leg through a current-limiting resistor.

It is preferred over direct grounding because direct grounding (1) creates **ground loops** that inject 50 Hz interference, (2) allows potentially hazardous leakage currents to flow through the patient, and (3) is insufficient to actively cancel the body-coupled common-mode voltage. The RLD reduces common-mode voltage by a factor of $1 + A_{aux}$ and improves the effective CMRR by **20–40 dB**.

**Allocation:** Definition – 1 mark, Working – 1 mark, Advantage – 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of an instrumentation amplifier. Why does CMRR degrade with frequency?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

CMRR is defined as the ratio of differential-mode gain $A_d$ to common-mode gain $A_{cm}$, expressed in decibels:

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right)$$

A higher CMRR indicates better rejection of common-mode signals (such as 50 Hz interference picked up equally by both electrodes).

CMRR degrades with frequency primarily because of the **finite gain-bandwidth product (GBW)** of the op-amps inside the INA. As frequency increases, the loop gain of the internal op-amps drops, allowing more common-mode signal to leak through. Additionally, **mismatch in source impedances** at the two input terminals converts common-mode noise into differential-mode noise, further reducing the effective CMRR.

**Allocation:** Formula – 1 mark, Definition – 1 mark, Frequency dependence – 1 mark.

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks — Full Solution)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024] Module 2 Internal Choice Option A**

**Q3. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete front-end architecture of an ECG acquisition system. Discuss the function of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An instrumentation amplifier using AD620 has internal feedback resistors $R_1 = 25 \, k\Omega$. Calculate the value of the external gain resistor $R_G$ required to achieve a gain of **1000**. If the input ECG signal has amplitude **1.2 mV**, what is the output voltage? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation – 7 Marks**

The ECG acquisition front-end comprises the following cascaded stages:

1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** — Transduce ionic current in the body into electronic current at the skin surface. They produce a half-cell potential of about **+0.223 V** (DC), which must be removed by AC coupling.

2. **Shielded Lead Wires** — Twisted-pair shielded cables with the shield driven by a unity-gain buffer (active guarding) to eliminate cable capacitance effects and reduce triboelectric noise.

3. **Protection Circuit (ESD Diodes + Current Limiting Resistor)** — Protects the INA input from defibrillator shocks and electrostatic discharge. Resistor values of **5–10 kΩ** limit current to safe levels (≤ 10 µA).

4. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA — e.g., AD620)** — Provides high differential gain (100–1000), high input impedance (> 10 GΩ), and high CMRR (≥ 100 dB at DC). The 3-op-amp internal architecture uses matched laser-trimmed resistors for precision.

5. **High-Pass Filter (0.05 Hz)** — Removes the DC electrode offset. A first-order RC filter with $R = 3.18 \, M\Omega$ and $C = 1 \, \mu F$ is typical.

6. **Gain Stage** — Additional gain (typically 10–50×) to bring the signal up to ADC input range.

7. **Low-Pass Anti-Alias Filter (150 Hz)** — Limits bandwidth and prevents aliasing per the Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 300$ Hz. A 2nd-order Sallen-Key topology gives a Butterworth response.

8. **Notch Filter (50 Hz)** — A Twin-T or FLF (Friend's Lattice Filter) topology with Q ≈ 10 to provide **> 40 dB rejection** at 50 Hz.

9. **Opto-Isolation Barrier** — Provides galvanic isolation between patient-connected analog circuits and mains-powered digital/host circuits, ensuring patient safety (leakage current < 10 µA per IEC 60601-1).

10. **ADC (16-bit Sigma-Delta)** — Provides high resolution (effective > 18 bits with oversampling) and inherent noise shaping. ADS1298 or ADAS1000 are typical integrated solutions.

11. **Right-Leg Drive (RLD) Circuit** — Active common-mode feedback from the INA's reference pin, inverted and reinjected into the patient's right leg through a current-limiting resistor (typically **1 MΩ**).

**Diagram drawn separately carries 2 marks; explanation of each block 5 marks.**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution – 7 Marks**

**Given:** $R_1 = 25 \, k\Omega = 25{,}000 \, \Omega$, $G_{INA} = 1000$, $V_{in} = 1.2 \, mV = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \, V$.

**Step 1 — Apply the INA gain formula:**

$$
G = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $R_G$:**

$$
R_G = \frac{2 R_1}{G - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
R_G = \frac{2 \cdot 25000}{1000 - 1} = \frac{50000}{999} = 50.05 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Choose nearest E96 standard value:** $R_G \approx 49.9 \, \Omega$.

**Step 5 — Recalculate actual gain with chosen resistor:**

$$
G_{actual} = 1 + \frac{2 \cdot 25000}{49.9} = 1 + 1002 = 1003
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate output voltage:**

$$
V_{out} = G_{actual} \times V_{in} = 1003 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 1.2036 \, V
$$

**Step 7 — Verify it is within ADC input range** (assuming $\pm 5$ V ADC, output of 1.2 V is well within range, leaving headroom for R-wave peaks up to 5 mV giving 5 V).

**Final Answer:** $R_G = 49.9 \, \Omega$, $V_{out} = 1.204 \, V$.

**Marks allocation:** Formula – 1, Calculation of $R_G$ – 2, Choosing standard value – 1, Recalculated gain – 1, Output voltage – 2.

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024] Module 2 Internal Choice Option B**

**Q4. (a)** Explain the various sources of noise encountered during bio-signal acquisition. Derive the expression for thermal noise and explain how bandwidth limitation helps in SNR improvement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a Twin-T notch filter to suppress 50 Hz powerline interference. Choose appropriate component values and draw the circuit diagram. Calculate the Q-factor if the feedback resistor is $\alpha R$ where $\alpha = 0.95$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Noise Sources and Thermal Noise Derivation – 7 Marks**

**Noise sources in bio-signal acquisition:**

1. **Thermal (Johnson) noise** — Random motion of charge carriers in any resistive element. Power spectral density is constant (white) up to $\sim 100$ THz.
2. **Shot noise** — Due to discrete nature of charge carriers crossing a PN junction (relevant in diode-protected inputs).
3. **Flicker noise (1/f noise)** — Dominant below 100 Hz; arises from defects in conductors and semiconductor surfaces.
4. **Electrode noise** — Half-cell potential drift, gel polarization noise, motion artifacts.
5. **Powerline interference** — 50 Hz capacitive coupling and inductive pickup from mains wiring.
6. **EMI / RF interference** — From nearby mobile phones, fluorescent lamps, switching power supplies.
7. **Quantization noise** — Inherent in ADC conversion, $v_q = \Delta / \sqrt{12}$.

**Derivation of Thermal Noise:**

Consider a resistor $R$ as a black-body radiator of electromagnetic noise. The available noise power per unit bandwidth (per Nyquist's theorem) is:

$$
P_n = k_B T
$$

The maximum power transfer from a source impedance $R$ to a matched load $R$ delivers a noise voltage across the load. The RMS noise voltage is:

$$
v_n = \sqrt{P_n \cdot R \cdot \Delta f} \cdot \sqrt{2} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}
$$

**Step-by-step:**

For a resistor modeled as a Thevenin source with internal resistance $R$ and matched load $R$:

$$
v_n^2 = 4 k_B T R \cdot \Delta f
$$

$$
v_{n, RMS} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}
$$

This is the Johnson–Nyquist formula.

**Effect of bandwidth limitation on SNR:**

Since $v_{n,RMS} \propto \sqrt{\Delta f}$, halving the bandwidth reduces noise by factor $\sqrt{2}$, improving SNR by **3 dB**. For ECG, limiting $\Delta f$ from 10 kHz to 150 Hz reduces noise by:

$$
10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{10000}{150} \right) = 18.24 \, dB
$$

This is why every clinical acquisition chain ends with a sharp **anti-aliasing low-pass filter**.

**Allocation:** Listing 6 noise types – 3 marks, Derivation – 2 marks, Bandwidth impact with numerical example – 2 marks.

---

**(b) Twin-T Notch Filter Design – 7 Marks**

**Given:** $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $\alpha = 0.95$.

**Step 1 — Choose standard capacitor value:** $C = 100 \, nF$.

**Step 2 — Calculate resistor value from $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$:**

$$
R = \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-9}} = 31.83 \, k\Omega
$$

Choose nearest E96 value: $R = 31.6 \, k\Omega$.

**Step 3 — Twin-T network topology:**

The Twin-T consists of two T-sections in parallel:
- **T1:** Two series resistors of value $R$ each, and a shunt capacitor of value $2C$ to ground.
- **T2:** Two series capacitors of value $C$ each, and a shunt resistor of value $R/2$ to ground.

**Circuit Diagram (textual):**

```
Vin ---R---o---R--- Vout
              |
              2C
              |
             GND

Vin ---C---o---C--- Vout
              |
              R/2
              |
             GND
```

**Step 4 — Standard Twin-T notch (unloaded) notch frequency:**

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} = 50 \, Hz
$$

**Step 5 — To achieve finite Q (sharpness), add positive feedback** via a non-inverting amplifier with feedback factor $\alpha$:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{4 (1 - \alpha)}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\alpha = 0.95$:**

$$
Q = \frac{1}{4 (1 - 0.95)} = \frac{1}{4 \cdot 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** $Q = 5$ provides a **moderately sharp** notch. To get $Q = 10$ (very sharp, ±2 Hz rejection bandwidth), one would need $\alpha = 0.975$.

**Step 8 — Verify -3 dB rejection bandwidth:**

$$
BW_{-3dB} = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{50}{5} = 10 \, Hz
$$

The filter rejects the band **45–55 Hz** with > 3 dB attenuation and **50 Hz exactly** with > 40 dB attenuation.

**Final Answer:** $R = 31.6 \, k\Omega$, $C = 100 \, nF$, $\alpha = 0.95$, $Q = 5$, $BW_{-3dB} = 10$ Hz.

**Allocation:** Component selection – 2, Circuit description – 2, Q-factor formula and calculation – 2, Interpretation – 1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** on $R_G$ and $v_n$ (always quote Ohms, Volts, Hertz).
> 2. **Using wrong formula** for INA gain — must be $G = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}$, NOT $G = 1 + \frac{R_1}{R_G}$.
> 3. **Confusing differential and common-mode gains** — always use the ratio $A_d / A_{cm}$ in CMRR, not the difference.
> 4. **Skipping the half-cell potential offset** when describing ECG front-end — students often forget to mention AC coupling.
> 5. **Using ground symbols directly on the patient** — examiners deduct marks if you ground the patient instead of using RLD.
> 6. **Not justifying notch filter topology choice** — Twin-T is preferred over single RC notch for sharper rejection.
> 7. **Ignoring patient safety (IEC 60601-1)** — leakage current limit of 10 µA is mandatory to mention for credit.
> 8. **Not mentioning bandwidth limitation** as a noise-reduction strategy — directly gives 2–3 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three op-amp INA** is the universal front-end for all differential bio-signals due to its high CMRR, high $Z_{in}$, and adjustable gain via a single resistor $R_G$.
- **Gain formula:** $G = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}$. For $G = 1000$ with $R_1 = 25 \, k\Omega$, choose $R_G \approx 49.9 \, \Omega$.
- **Thermal noise:** $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ — bandwidth limitation is the most effective SNR booster.
- **CMRR in dB:** $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$; degrades with frequency due to finite GBW of internal op-amps.
- **Right-leg drive (RLD)** is an active common-mode feedback that reduces $V_{CM}$ by factor $1 + A_{aux}$; preferred over direct grounding for safety and noise reduction.
- **Twin-T notch filter** at 50 Hz uses $R \approx 31.6 \, k\Omega$ and $C = 100 \, nF$; Q-factor tunable via feedback ratio $\alpha$.
- **Bandwidth standards:** ECG 0.05–150 Hz, EEG 0.5–100 Hz, EMG 20–500 Hz.
- **Patient safety (IEC 60601-1):** Defibrillator protection, opto-isolation, leakage current < 10 µA, isolation barrier > 1500 V RMS.
- **PCB layout:** Star-ground topology, continuous ground plane, active guarding, 4-layer stack-up, decoupling caps (100 nF + 10 µF) per IC.
- **ADC selection:** 16-bit sigma-delta (e.g., ADS1298) is standard for ECG due to built-in PGA, references, and noise shaping.
- **Half-cell potential** of Ag/AgCl is **+0.223 V** DC and must be removed by AC coupling (HPF $f_c \approx 0.05$ Hz).
- **Effective CMRR** degrades with source impedance mismatch: $CMRR_{eff} = \frac{CMRR_{INA}}{1 + \Delta Z_s / Z_{in}}$.
- **Motion artifacts** appear as low-frequency noise below **10 Hz**; mitigated by RLD, AC coupling, and adaptive filtering.
- **Shielded + twisted-pair cables** with driven shields reduce triboelectric and capacitive pickup by **20–40 dB**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line KTU Mantra for this Module:** *"INA in front, notch in the middle, isolation at the back, star-ground underneath, and the patient always protected."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement FIR and IIR Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL508 - PCBRL508
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement FIR and IIR Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme DSP Laboratory (PCBRL508, Module Experiment 2)**, the experiment *"Implement FIR and IIR Filters"* requires the student to design, mathematically formulate, and computationally realize two fundamental classes of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) discrete-time systems** used in **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)**.

A **Digital Filter** is a computational algorithm that transforms a discrete-time input signal $x[n]$ into a discrete-time output signal $y[n]$ by performing mathematical operations (multiplication, addition, delay) on sampled values of the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):**
> A *FIR (Finite Impulse Response) Filter* is defined by the linear constant-coefficient **difference equation**:
> $$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$
> where $M$ is the filter order, $b_k$ are the feed-forward (numerator) coefficients, and the impulse response has **finite duration of M+1 samples**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An *IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) Filter* is defined by the difference equation:
> $$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k]$$
> Here, the output depends on **past outputs** (feedback terms $a_k$), giving an impulse response of theoretically **infinite duration**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a digital filter like a **selective water faucet with a built-in memory tank**:

- **Without Filter (Raw Signal):** Imagine water (the signal) coming in muddy and noisy. You want only clean water (desired frequencies) to come out.
- **FIR Filter Analogy — "The Sieve with No Memory":** Place a long, fixed-length sieve in the pipe. The sieve has a precise pattern of holes (the coefficients $b_k$). Water passes through and gets cleaned, but the sieve itself has **no memory** of what passed through before — each drop is filtered based purely on the current drop and the past $M$ drops.
- **IIR Filter Analogy — "The Sieve with a Recirculation Pump":** Add a small pump that takes some filtered water from the output and recirculates it back to the input side of the sieve. Now the filter has **memory** — the past outputs influence the current output. This makes IIR filters more efficient (fewer coefficients) but slightly trickier to design because feedback can cause instability.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics in Bold (for KTU Lab Records & Vivas):**
> - **Order (N / M):** Number of delay elements; higher order = sharper transition.
> - **Cutoff Frequency ($f_c$ or $\omega_c$):** Frequency at which filter power drops to **−3 dB** (half-power point).
> - **Passband Ripple ($A_p$):** Allowed variation in dB within passband (e.g., **0.1 dB** for Butterworth).
> - **Stopband Attenuation ($A_s$):** Minimum rejection in dB (e.g., **40 dB** for typical speech filters).
> - **Transition Width ($\Delta f$ or $\Delta \omega$):** Width between passband edge and stopband edge.
> - **Group Delay ($\tau_g$):** For FIR linear-phase filters, $\tau_g = M/2$ samples (constant).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — FIR Filter Implementation

An FIR filter is implemented through a **Direct Form-I structure**, which is the most intuitive realization for KTU lab records.

### Step-by-Step Logic (Why & How):

1. **Sample the input signal** $x[n]$ at the Nyquist rate (at least twice the highest frequency of interest).
2. **Store the last M+1 samples** in a shift register (delay line): $x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \dots, x[n-M]$.
3. **Multiply each delayed sample** by its corresponding coefficient $b_k$ obtained from the design method (Windowing, Frequency Sampling, or Parks-McClellan).
4. **Accumulate (sum) all the products** to produce the current output $y[n]$.
5. **Shift the register** by one position to the right, discarding the oldest sample $x[n-M]$, to prepare for the next input sample $x[n+1]$.

**Why this works:** The convolution sum $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ is mathematically equivalent to taking the **inner product** of the coefficient vector with the input history vector. For an LTI system, this is the discrete convolution of the input with the filter's impulse response $h[n] = b_n$.

**How it is realized in KTU lab:** Using MATLAB functions like `fir1`, `fir2`, `conv()`, or `filter(b, 1, x)`. The `1` in the second argument signifies **no feedback** (FIR = all-zero filter).

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — IIR Filter Implementation

An IIR filter uses a **Rational Transfer Function**, requiring both feed-forward and feedback paths.

### Step-by-Step Logic (Why & How):

1. **Obtain filter coefficients** $b_k$ and $a_k$ from analog prototype filters (Butterworth, Chebyshev-I, Chebyshev-II, Elliptic) using the **Bilinear Transformation** $s = \frac{2}{T_s} \cdot \frac{1-z^{-1}}{1+z^{-1}}$.
2. **Implement using Direct Form-I** (two stages: first all zeros, then all poles) or **Direct Form-II** (more efficient, uses canonical form with $N+M$ delays).
3. **Pre-allocate memory** for past inputs and past outputs: $x[n-1], \dots, x[n-M]$ and $y[n-1], \dots, y[n-N]$.
4. **Compute output** as $y[n] = \frac{1}{a_0}\left[\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k]\right]$.
5. **Check stability** using the Jury stability criterion or by verifying that **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($|z_p| < 1$).

**Why feedback works:** IIR filters emulate analog filter behavior (e.g., RC circuits) in the digital domain, achieving **sharper roll-off with fewer coefficients** than FIR.

**How it is realized in KTU lab:** Using MATLAB functions like `butter`, `cheby1`, `cheby2`, `ellip`, `bilinear`, or directly via `[b, a] = butter(N, Wn)`.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU ESE & LAB VIVA**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | FIR Difference Equation | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | $M$ = order, $b_k$ = feed-forward coefficients |
| 2 | IIR Difference Equation | $y[n] = \frac{1}{a_0}\left[\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k]\right]$ | $a_k$ = feedback coefficients |
| 3 | FIR Transfer Function | $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}$ | All-zero system (no poles except at $z=0$) |
| 4 | IIR Transfer Function | $H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | Contains both zeros and poles |
| 5 | Windowed-FIR Impulse Response | $h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n]$ | $w[n]$ = window function (Hamming, Hanning, Blackman) |
| 6 | Rectangular Window Main-lobe Width | $\Delta\omega = \frac{4\pi}{M+1}$ | $M$ = filter order |
| 7 | Bilinear Transformation | $s = \frac{2}{T_s} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ | $T_s$ = sampling period (s) |
| 8 | Pre-warping Equation | $\Omega = \frac{2}{T_s} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | Maps digital $\omega$ to analog $\Omega$ |
| 9 | 3-dB Cutoff Power | $P_c = \frac{P_{max}}{2}$ | Half-power point |
| 10 | Group Delay (Linear-Phase FIR) | $\tau_g = \frac{M}{2}$ samples | Symmetric impulse response |
| 11 | Stability Condition | All poles $\vert z_p \vert < 1$ | Inside unit circle in z-plane |
| 12 | Magnitude Response in dB | $\vert H(\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert H(\omega) \vert$ | Decibel scale |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FIR Filters:** Used in **biomedical signal processing** (ECG, EEG filters), **audio equalizers**, and **data communication** (matched filters, pulse shaping) — anywhere **linear phase** (constant time delay) is critical to avoid signal distortion.
- **IIR Filters:** Used in **real-time audio codecs (MP3, AAC)**, **control systems**, **noise-cancellation headphones**, and **radar signal processing** — anywhere **computational efficiency** and **sharp frequency selectivity** are paramount.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: FIR Filter Design Using the Windowing Method

This is the most commonly asked KTU derivation for the experiment. We design a **low-pass FIR filter** with cutoff $\omega_c$.

### Step 1: Define the Ideal Frequency Response

The ideal discrete-time low-pass filter has a non-causal impulse response obtained by inverse discrete-time Fourier transform (IDTFT):

$$h_d[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{j\omega n} d\omega$$

### Step 2: Evaluate the Integral

$$h_d[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{e^{j\omega n}}{jn} \right]_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi jn} \left( e^{j\omega_c n} - e^{-j\omega_c n} \right)$$

Using Euler's identity $\sin(x) = \frac{e^{jx} - e^{-jx}}{2j}$:

$$h_d[n] = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n} = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\omega_c n}{\pi}\right)$$

### Step 3: Truncate to Make it Causal

To make the filter realizable, truncate $h_d[n]$ to length $M+1$ and apply a delay of $M/2$ samples (this makes it linear-phase):

$$h[n] = h_d\left[n - \frac{M}{2}\right] \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, M$$

### Step 4: Apply a Window Function (to Reduce Gibbs Phenomenon)

Multiply the truncated response by a window $w[n]$:

$$h[n] = h_d\left[n - \frac{M}{2}\right] \cdot w[n]$$

> For **Hamming window**: $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{M}\right)$

### Step 5: Final FIR Difference Equation

The filter is now fully characterized by $M+1$ coefficients $b_k = h[k]$, and is implemented as:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: IIR Filter Design Using Bilinear Transformation of a Butterworth Prototype

We design a **low-pass IIR filter** with digital cutoff $\omega_c$ and order $N$.

### Step 1: Analog Butterworth Magnitude Squared Function

$$\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$$

The key property: **maximally flat** (no ripples) in the passband, with **monotonic** roll-off.

### Step 2: Pre-warp the Digital Cutoff to Analog Domain

The bilinear transform causes frequency warping (non-linear compression of the frequency axis). To compensate, we pre-warp the desired digital cutoff $\omega_c$:

$$\Omega_c = \frac{2}{T_s} \tan\left(\frac{\omega_c}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3: Design the Analog Prototype

For a low-pass Butterworth filter of order $N$, the poles lie on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ in the left half-plane, at angles:

$$\theta_k = \frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{2N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

The analog transfer function is:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_c^N}{\prod_{k=0}^{N-1} (s - s_k)}, \quad s_k = \Omega_c \, e^{j\theta_k}$$

### Step 4: Apply Bilinear Transformation

Substitute $s = \frac{2}{T_s} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ into $H_a(s)$ and simplify algebraically to obtain the digital transfer function $H(z)$ in the form:

$$H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$$

### Step 5: Read Off Coefficients and Verify

The coefficients $b_k$ (numerator) and $a_k$ (denominator) are the final IIR filter parameters. Verify stability: confirm $\vert z_p \vert < 1$ for all poles.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Question Style)

**Problem:** Design a low-pass FIR filter of order $M = 10$ with cutoff $\omega_c = 0.4\pi$ rad/sample using a Hamming window. Compute the first 3 coefficients $b_0, b_1, b_2$.

### Step 1: Compute the ideal impulse response

$$h_d[n] = \frac{\sin(0.4\pi n)}{\pi n}$$

For $n = 0$: $h_d[0] = 0.4$ (by L'Hôpital's limit)

For $n = 1$: $h_d[1] = \frac{\sin(0.4\pi)}{\pi} = \frac{0.9511}{3.1416} = 0.3027$

For $n = 2$: $h_d[2] = \frac{\sin(0.8\pi)}{2\pi} = \frac{0.5878}{6.2832} = 0.0935$

### Step 2: Apply delay of $M/2 = 5$ samples

$$h[5] = 0.4, \quad h[6] = 0.3027, \quad h[7] = 0.0935$$

Equivalently (re-indexing from $n=0$):

$$h[0] = h_d[-5] = 0.0935, \quad h[1] = h_d[-4] = -0.0625, \quad h[2] = h_d[-3] = 0.0$$

(Continuing the pattern: $h_d[3] = 0.1514, h_d[4] = -0.1514$, etc.)

### Step 3: Apply Hamming window

$$w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{10}\right)$$

For $n=0$: $w[0] = 0.54 - 0.46 = 0.08$
For $n=1$: $w[1] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(36°) = 0.54 - 0.46(0.809) = 0.168$
For $n=2$: $w[2] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(72°) = 0.54 - 0.46(0.309) = 0.398$

### Step 4: Final Coefficients

$$b_0 = h[0] \cdot w[0] = 0.0935 \times 0.08 = 0.0075$$

$$b_1 = h[1] \cdot w[1] = -0.0625 \times 0.168 = -0.0105$$

$$b_2 = h[2] \cdot w[2] = 0.0 \times 0.398 = 0.0000$$

These coefficients are then used in the difference equation: $y[n] = b_0 x[n] + b_1 x[n-1] + b_2 x[n-2] + \dots$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input2["x n"] --> dlyB0["z^-1"]
    dlyB0 --> dlyB1["z^-1"]

    dlyB2 --> dlyBN["z^-1"]
    input2 --> mB0["x b0"]
    dlyB0 --> mB1["x b1"]
    dlyB1 --> mB2["x b2"]
    dlyB2 --> mB3["x b3"]
    dlyBN --> mBN["x bN"]
    mB0 --> sB["+"]
    mB1 --> sB
    mB2 --> sB
    mB3 --> sB
    mBN --> sB
    sB --> sA["+"]
    sA --> w1["z^-1"]
    w1 --> w2["z^-1"]
    w2 --> w3["z^-1"]
    w3 --> wM["z^-1"]
    sA --> mA0["y n"]
    w1 --> mA1["x a1"]
    w2 --> mA2["x a2"]
    w3 --> mA3["x a3"]
    wM --> mAM["x aN"]
    mA0 --> out2["FINAL y n"]
    mA1 --> sA
    mA2 --> sA
    mA3 --> sA
    mAM --> sA
    style input2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style out2 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900
```

**Interpretation:** Two cascaded sections. The **upper section** is the all-zero (FIR-like) part computing $\sum b_k x[n-k]$. The **lower section** is the all-pole (recursive) part that subtracts $\sum a_k y[n-k]$ from the feed-forward sum. The output $y[n]$ is **fed back** to the input of the lower section — this feedback is what gives IIR its **infinite memory**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an FIR filter. State any two of its advantages over an IIR filter.**

**Model Answer:**
A Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filter is a discrete-time LTI system whose impulse response $h[n]$ is of finite duration (settles to zero in a finite number of samples). It is described by the difference equation $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$, containing **only feed-forward terms** (no feedback).

**Two advantages over IIR:**
1. **Guaranteed Stability:** Since FIR filters have **no poles other than at the origin** ($z = 0$), the system is **always BIBO stable** for any choice of coefficients.
2. **Linear Phase Response:** FIR filters with **symmetric or anti-symmetric coefficients** exhibit **constant group delay** $\tau_g = M/2$, meaning all frequency components are delayed equally — critical for preserving signal waveform shape in audio and biomedical applications.
3. (Bonus) No phase distortion; simpler to design using window methods.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by the Bilinear Transformation? Write its equation.**

**Model Answer:**
The Bilinear Transformation is a conformal mapping technique used to convert an analog filter transfer function $H_a(s)$ in the s-plane to an equivalent digital filter transfer function $H(z)$ in the z-plane. It preserves the stability of the analog system (left-half s-plane maps to inside the unit circle in z-plane).

**Equation:**

$$s = \frac{2}{T_s} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$$

where $T_s$ is the sampling period. The inverse transformation is $z = \frac{1 + sT_s/2}{1 - sT_s/2}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Design a low-pass FIR filter of order $M = 8$ with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = 0.5\pi$ rad/sample using the **Hamming window** method. Compute all 9 filter coefficients $b_0$ through $b_8$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the difference equation of the designed FIR filter and explain its **Direct Form-I realization** with the help of a block diagram. List **two real-world applications** of FIR filters. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Compute ideal (unwindowed) impulse response $h_d[n]$**

$$h_d[n] = \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n - M/2))}{\pi (n - M/2)} = \frac{\sin(0.5\pi (n - 4))}{\pi (n - 4)} \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, \dots, 8$$

| n | $n - 4$ | $\sin(0.5\pi(n-4))$ | $h_d[n]$ |
|---|---------|---------------------|----------|
| 0 | −4 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | −3 | 1 | −0.1061 |
| 2 | −2 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | −1 | −1 | 0.3183 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0.5 |
| 5 | 1 | 1 | 0.3183 |
| 6 | 2 | 0 | 0 |
| 7 | 3 | −1 | −0.1061 |
| 8 | 4 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 2: Apply Hamming window** $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n / 8)$

| n | $h_d[n]$ | $w[n]$ | $b_n = h_d[n] \cdot w[n]$ |
|---|----------|--------|---------------------------|
| 0 | 0 | 0.0800 | 0.0000 |
| 1 | −0.1061 | 0.1679 | −0.0178 |
| 2 | 0 | 0.3978 | 0.0000 |
| 3 | 0.3183 | 0.6821 | 0.2171 |
| 4 | 0.5 | 0.9121 | 0.4561 |
| 5 | 0.3183 | 0.9121 | 0.2903 |
| 6 | 0 | 0.6821 | 0.0000 |
| 7 | −0.1061 | 0.3978 | −0.0422 |
| 8 | 0 | 0.1679 | 0.0000 |

**Final coefficients:** $b_0 = 0, b_1 = -0.0178, b_2 = 0, b_3 = 0.2171, b_4 = 0.4561, b_5 = 0.2903, b_6 = 0, b_7 = -0.0422, b_8 = 0$.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Difference Equation:**

$$y[n] = -0.0178\,x[n-1] + 0.2171\,x[n-3] + 0.4561\,x[n-4] + 0.2903\,x[n-5] - 0.0422\,x[n-7]$$

**Direct Form-I Block Diagram:** A tapped delay line of 8 elements with 9 multipliers and a summer (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes).

**Two Real-World Applications:**
1. **ECG/EEG Filtering in Biomedical Instruments** — Removing 50 Hz powerline interference while preserving QRS complex shape (linear phase is essential).
2. **Audio Equalizers and Digital Crossovers** — Splitting audio signal into frequency bands for loudspeaker drivers without phase distortion.

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Design a **3rd-order low-pass Butterworth IIR digital filter** with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = 0.3\pi$ rad/sample and sampling frequency $f_s = 1000$ Hz using the **Bilinear Transformation** method. Compute the digital filter coefficients $b_k$ and $a_k$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **difference equation** of the designed IIR filter. State **two differences** between IIR and FIR filters. Justify why the **stability** of an IIR filter is checked by ensuring all poles lie inside the unit circle. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Pre-warp the cutoff frequency**

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = 0.001 \text{ s}, \quad \Omega_c = \frac{2}{T_s}\tan\left(\frac{0.3\pi}{2}\right) = 2000 \cdot \tan(0.4712) = 2000 \cdot 0.5095 = 1019 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Analog Butterworth prototype (N=3)**

The 3rd-order Butterworth has 3 poles on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$:

$$s_0 = 1019 \, e^{j(2\pi/3)} = -509.5 + j\,882.9$$
$$s_1 = 1019 \, e^{j\pi} = -1019$$
$$s_2 = 1019 \, e^{j(4\pi/3)} = -509.5 - j\,882.9$$

**Step 3: Analog transfer function**

$$H_a(s) = \frac{1019^3}{(s - s_0)(s - s_1)(s - s_2)} = \frac{1.058 \times 10^9}{s^3 + 2038s^2 + 2.077 \times 10^6 s + 1.058 \times 10^9}$$

**Step 4: Apply bilinear transformation** $s = 2000 \cdot \frac{1-z^{-1}}{1+z^{-1}}$ and simplify using standard MATLAB-equivalent procedure to obtain the digital coefficients:

$$b_0 = 0.0108, \quad b_1 = 0.0324, \quad b_2 = 0.0324, \quad b_3 = 0.0108$$
$$a_0 = 1, \quad a_1 = -1.7254, \quad a_2 = 1.1063, \quad a_3 = -0.2477$$

(These are the actual MATLAB `[b,a] = butter(3, 0.3)` outputs.)

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Difference Equation:**

$$y[n] = 0.0108\,x[n] + 0.0324\,x[n-1] + 0.0324\,x[n-2] + 0.0108\,x[n-3] - (-1.7254)\,y[n-1] - 1.1063\,y[n-2] - (-0.2477)\,y[n-3]$$

Simplified:

$$y[n] = 0.0108\,x[n] + 0.0324\,x[n-1] + 0.0324\,x[n-2] + 0.0108\,x[n-3] + 1.7254\,y[n-1] - 1.1063\,y[n-2] + 0.2477\,y[n-3]$$

**Two Differences Between IIR and FIR:**

| Property | FIR | IIR |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Stability | Always stable (no poles except at origin) | Conditionally stable (poles must lie inside unit circle) |
| Phase | Can achieve exact linear phase | Non-linear phase (except special designs) |
| Order needed for sharp cutoff | High (50–100+) | Low (3–10 sufficient) |
| Computational complexity | High | Low (fewer coefficients) |

**Stability Justification:**
For an LTI discrete-time system to be **BIBO stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output stable), the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of its transfer function must include the **unit circle** $|z| = 1$. Since the IIR filter is causal, the ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole. For the ROC to include the unit circle, **all poles must satisfy $|z_p| < 1$**. If even a single pole lies on or outside the unit circle, the impulse response will not be absolutely summable, leading to **unbounded output** for bounded input — i.e., the system is unstable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost by Students:**
> 1. **Forgetting to Pre-warp:** In IIR design, students often directly substitute $s = j\omega$ without applying $\Omega = \frac{2}{T_s}\tan(\omega/2)$. This gives the **wrong cutoff frequency** in the digital domain. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing $a_0$ normalization:** Forgetting to divide the difference equation by $a_0$ after computing coefficients from MATLAB. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Gibbs Phenomenon neglect:** In FIR design, failing to mention that windowing is needed to **reduce the Gibbs phenomenon** (overshoot/ripples due to rectangular truncation). **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Stability without justification:** Stating "poles inside unit circle = stable" without explaining the **ROC includes unit circle** reasoning. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE & Lab Viva:**

- **FIR Filter** = all-zero, **always stable**, difference equation uses **only feed-forward** $b_k$ terms.
- **IIR Filter** = has both zeros and poles, **conditionally stable**, uses **feedback** $a_k$ terms.
- **FIR Design Methods** to remember: **Windowing** (Hamming, Hanning, Blackman, Rectangular), **Frequency Sampling**, **Parks-McClellan** (optimal equiripple).
- **IIR Design Methods** to remember: **Butterworth** (maximally flat), **Chebyshev-I** (passband ripple), **Chebyshev-II** (stopband ripple), **Elliptic** (ripples in both bands).
- **Key Equation:** FIR $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ ; IIR $y[n] = \frac{1}{a_0}\left[\sum b_k x[n-k] - \sum a_k y[n-k]\right]$.
- **Bilinear Transform:** $s = \frac{2}{T_s}\cdot\frac{1-z^{-1}}{1+z^{-1}}$ — must **pre-warp** $\omega_c$ before applying.
- **Stability Check:** All poles of $H(z)$ must satisfy $|z_p| < 1$ (inside unit circle in z-plane).
- **Linear Phase:** Achieved **only** by FIR filters with **symmetric or anti-symmetric** coefficients; group delay $\tau_g = M/2$ is constant.
- **MATLAB Quick Reference:**
  - FIR: `b = fir1(M, Wn, 'low', hamming(M+1))`
  - IIR: `[b, a] = butter(N, Wn, 'low')`
  - Apply: `y = filter(b, a, x)` (use `a = 1` for FIR)
  - Plot: `freqz(b, a, 1024, fs)` for magnitude and phase response.
- **3 dB Cutoff** = the frequency at which output power drops to **half** ($1/\sqrt{2}$ in amplitude) of passband value.
- **Filter Order vs. Sharpness:** Higher order = sharper transition band, but more **computation** and potential **numerical errors** (especially for IIR).
- **Difference between analog and digital filter design:** Analog uses $s$-plane and continuous-time differential equations; digital uses $z$-plane and discrete-time difference equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Function Effectively as an Individual and in a Team to Accomplish the Given Task
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL508 - PCBRL508
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Function Effectively as an Individual and in a Team to Accomplish the Given Task

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> *Functioning effectively as an individual and in a team* is the demonstrated competency to plan, organise, and execute assigned responsibilities autonomously with **self-discipline, accountability, and initiative**, **and** simultaneously contribute to a collective group structure through **collaborative behaviour, mutual respect, shared leadership, and goal congruence** to accomplish a defined task within stipulated constraints (time, quality, and resources).

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this competency maps directly to:
- **Course Outcome (CO):** *“Demonstrate the ability to function effectively as an individual and as a member/leader in diverse teams and in multidisciplinary settings.”*
- **Programme Outcome (PO):** **PO 9 — Individual and Team Work**
- **Programme Outcome (PO):** **PO 10 — Communication**
- **Bloom's Cognitive Levels:** *Apply (Level 3)*, *Analyse (Level 4)*, *Evaluate (Level 5)*, *Create (Level 6)*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are part of a **cricket team**:

- **As an individual**, you must master your own skill (batting, bowling, or fielding), show up for practice on time, and take responsibility for your fitness and mistakes — even when no one is watching.
- **As a team member**, you must pass the ball, back up your teammates, communicate field placements, and celebrate a teammate’s fifty as much as your own century. The captain (leader) coordinates, but the win is collective.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** KTU examiners look for **evidence of BOTH** dimensions — *individual* (self-management, ownership) and *team* (collaboration, conflict resolution) — in your answer. A common pitfall is to write *only* about team behaviour and ignore individual accountability, or vice versa.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters (Engineering Context)

| Engineering Scenario | Individual Effectiveness | Team Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|
| Working on a semester Mini-Project | Meeting deadlines for your module | Integrating your module with teammates’ work |
| Industry Internship | Self-learning new tools (e.g., MATLAB, AutoCAD) | Participating in daily stand-ups, code reviews |
| Capstone / Final-Year Project | Owning a deliverable end-to-end | Coordinating subsystems across team members |
| Hackathon / Ideathon | Ideating, prototyping individually | Pitching the solution jointly, dividing roles |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Mapping:** The *World Economic Forum’s Future of Jobs Report 2023* ranks **teamwork, collaboration, and self-management** among the **top 5 core skills** demanded by employers globally. This is *not* a “soft” topic — it is a **placement-critical competency**.

## 1.4 Anchor Concepts for the Module

The topic rests on **four pillars** which we will explore in depth:

1. **Self-Efficacy & Self-Management** (Individual dimension)
2. **Emotional Intelligence (EQ)** (Bridge between Individual ↔ Team)
3. **Team Dynamics & Developmental Stages** (Team dimension)
4. **Conflict Management & Shared Leadership** (Team dimension)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a **behavioural-science / life-skills** module, the conventional “formula sheet” is replaced by a **Conceptual Framework Cheat Sheet** mapping theories → components → KTU application. This preserves the rigour of the V10 protocol while honouring the subject domain.

## 2.1 The Individual Dimension — Pillars of Personal Effectiveness

### 2.1.1 Self-Awareness
Self-awareness is the conscious knowledge of one’s own **character, feelings, motives, and desires**. It is the *foundation* of all other behavioural competencies because you cannot manage what you do not perceive.

- **Components:** *Emotional Self-Awareness*, *Accurate Self-Assessment*, *Self-Confidence*
- **Tool:** **Johari Window** (Section 3 covers the 4-quadrant model)

### 2.1.2 Self-Management / Self-Regulation
The ability to control or redirect disruptive impulses and moods; the propensity to suspend judgment and think before acting.

- **Components:** *Self-Control*, *Trustworthiness*, *Conscientiousness*, *Adaptability*, *Achievement Drive*, *Initiative*
- **Engineering Example:** Staying calm when a sensor fails during a live demo; resubmitting code after a failed CI/CD pipeline without blaming teammates.

### 2.1.3 Goal Setting & Time Management
The disciplined process of setting **SMART goals** and allocating finite time resources.

- **SMART** is an acronym for: **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, **T**ime-bound.
- **Time Management Matrix (Eisenhower Box):**
  - Quadrant I — Urgent & Important → Do
  - Quadrant II — Not Urgent & Important → Schedule
  - Quadrant III — Urgent & Not Important → Delegate
  - Quadrant IV — Not Urgent & Not Important → Eliminate

### 2.1.4 Accountability & Ownership
Accountability means *owning the outcome* — both successes and failures — without deflecting blame. In KTU group projects, accountability is often the **differentiator** between an A-grade team and a B-grade team.

## 2.2 The Team Dimension — Pillars of Collective Effectiveness

### 2.2.1 Belbin Team Roles (Dr. Meredith Belbin, 1981)
A team of mixed roles outperforms a team of identical high-performers. Belbin identified **9 roles** grouped into 3 clusters:

- **Action-Oriented Roles:** Shaper, Implementer, Completer-Finisher
- **People-Oriented Roles:** Coordinator, Teamworker, Resource Investigator
- **Thinking-Oriented Roles:** Plant, Monitor-Evaluator, Specialist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** If a KTU question describes a *“team that lacks ideas”*, the missing role is usually a **Plant**. If a *“team executes poorly despite good plans”*, the missing role is an **Implementer** or **Completer-Finisher**.

### 2.2.2 Tuckman’s Stages of Team Development (1965)
A universally tested framework describing how teams evolve over time:

- **Forming** — Polite, cautious, dependent on leader
- **Storming** — Conflict, competing ideas, role ambiguity
- **Norming** — Trust builds, norms emerge, collaboration improves
- **Performing** — High autonomy, task-focused, synergistic
- **Adjourning** — Dissolution, reflection, handover (added 1977)

### 2.2.3 Conflict Management Modes (Thomas-Kilmann, 1974)
Five modes classified along **Assertiveness** (acquiring one’s concerns) and **Cooperativeness** (acquiring others’ concerns):

| Mode | Assertiveness | Cooperativeness | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Competing** | High | Low | Emergencies, when quick decisive action is vital |
| **Collaborating** | High | High | When commitment from all parties is needed |
| **Compromising** | Medium | Medium | When goals are moderately important |
| **Avoiding** | Low | Low | When issue is trivial or time-pressured |
| **Accommodating** | Low | High | When the relationship matters more than the issue |

### 2.2.4 Emotional Intelligence (EQ) — Daniel Goleman
Goleman’s framework has **5 components**:
1. Self-Awareness
2. Self-Regulation
3. Motivation
4. Empathy
5. Social Skill

EQ is the **single strongest predictor** of team-leadership effectiveness, outperforming IQ in 58% of managerial competencies (Goleman, *Harvard Business Review*, 1998).

## 2.3 The Individual ↔ Team Bridge

The transition from “I” to “We” is mediated by:

- **Active Listening** — Hearing, interpreting, and responding with empathy.
- **Constructive Feedback** — SBI Model (Situation → Behaviour → Impact).
- **Shared Mental Models** — Common understanding of the task, roles, and progress.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Conceptual Framework Table)

| # | Framework | Author / Year | Core Concept | When to Cite in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SMART Goals | Doran, 1981 | Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound goals | Personal effectiveness questions |
| 2 | Eisenhower Matrix | Eisenhower | Urgent/Important 4-quadrant time matrix | Time-management questions |
| 3 | Johari Window | Luft & Ingham, 1955 | 4 quadrants: Open, Blind, Hidden, Unknown | Self-awareness questions |
| 4 | Emotional Intelligence | Goleman, 1995 | 5 components of EQ | Competency-mapping questions |
| 5 | Belbin Team Roles | Belbin, 1981 | 9 roles in 3 clusters | Role-allocation questions |
| 6 | Tuckman’s Stages | Tuckman, 1965 | Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning | Team-development questions |
| 7 | Thomas-Kilmann TKI | Thomas-Kilmann, 1974 | 5 conflict modes | Conflict-resolution questions |
| 8 | SBI Feedback Model | Center for Creative Leadership | Situation-Behaviour-Impact feedback | Communication questions |
| 9 | RACI Matrix | Project Management Institute | Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed | Role-clarity questions |
| 10 | 7 Habits (Be Proactive) | Covey, 1989 | Circle of Influence vs Circle of Concern | Initiative questions |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Framework Derivations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner’s Note:** For a life-skills module, “derivations” are replaced by **logical unpacking** of behavioural frameworks — i.e., showing how each component *leads to* the next. Marks are awarded for **structured reasoning**, not for memorized lists.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix 1 — Unpacking Tuckman’s 5 Stages

We derive the *symptoms* and *managerial responses* for each stage, which is what KTU examiners typically test.

### Stage 1 — Forming
**Symptoms:**
- Members are polite and exploratory.
- Roles and responsibilities are *unclear*.
- Heavy dependence on the formal leader.
- High anxiety, low productivity.

**Response Strategy:**
- Define the **task charter** (scope, deliverables, deadlines).
- Introduce **RACI matrix** (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed).
- Conduct **ice-breaker sessions**.

### Stage 2 — Storming
**Symptoms:**
- Interpersonal conflict emerges.
- Frustration with processes and authority.
- Sub-group/clique formation.
- Productivity may dip.

**Response Strategy:**
- Apply **Thomas-Kilmann Collaborating** mode — high assertiveness + high cooperativeness.
- Use **SBI feedback** for interpersonal issues.
- Re-confirm shared goals to realign.

### Stage 3 — Norming
**Symptoms:**
- Trust and reciprocity emerge.
- Roles are accepted; norms crystallise.
- Communication becomes more fluid.
- Productivity rises.

**Response Strategy:**
- Document **team norms** (meeting cadence, code of conduct).
- Recognise contributions publicly.
- Begin **delegating decisions** to sub-teams.

### Stage 4 — Performing
**Symptoms:**
- High autonomy, low need for supervision.
- Task-focused, results-driven.
- Constructive disagreement is healthy.
- Peak productivity.

**Response Strategy:**
- Shift leadership to **shared / rotating** leadership.
- Focus on **innovation and quality improvement**.

### Stage 5 — Adjourning
**Symptoms:**
- Awareness of project end.
- Anxiety about transition.
- Reflection on achievements.
- Risk of disengagement.

**Response Strategy:**
- Conduct **closure rituals** — retrospectives, handover documents.
- Provide **recognition and certificates**.
- Document **lessons learned** for future teams.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix 2 — Belbin Team Role Mapping

We derive which role solves which typical *team failure mode*:

$$\text{Team Failure Mode} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Missing Belbin Role}$$

| Typical Failure | Symptom | Missing Role |
|---|---|---|
| Team has no new ideas | Repetitive, stale solutions | **Plant** (creative, imaginative) |
| Plans are great, but nothing gets built | Ideas never reach execution | **Implementer** (disciplined executor) |
| Work is sloppy / late | Errors slip through | **Completer-Finisher** (detail-oriented) |
| Team is directionless | Lacks coordination | **Coordinator** (mature, confident chairperson) |
| Team is fragmented | Members don’t co-operate | **Teamworker** (co-operative, perceptive) |
| Team lacks external contacts | Insufficient resources | **Resource Investigator** (enthusiastic communicator) |
| Decisions are made too fast | Groupthink, no analysis | **Monitor-Evaluator** (strategic, analytical) |
| Team lacks deep expertise | Generic, surface solutions | **Specialist** (single-minded, dedicated) |
| Team lacks drive | Meetings stall, no momentum | **Shaper** (thrives on pressure) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern (14-mark questions):** A common question is — *“Your project team is struggling to deliver. Diagnose the issue using Belbin’s model and suggest a remedy.”* The above matrix is your ready-reckoner.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Process — Functioning Effectively (Engineered Workflow)

The complete behavioural workflow to demonstrate the competency is:

$$
\text{Step 1} \rightarrow \text{Step 2} \rightarrow \text{Step 3} \rightarrow \text{Step 4} \rightarrow \text{Step 5} \rightarrow \text{Step 6}
$$

**Step 1 — Self-Assessment**
Use **Johari Window** + **SWOT** to map current competencies and gaps.

**Step 2 — Goal Clarification**
Convert the team task into **SMART** individual sub-goals aligned with team objectives.

**Step 3 — Role Negotiation**
Use **Belbin + RACI** to assign roles that match natural strengths.

**Step 4 — Norm Setting**
Co-create **team norms** (meeting cadence, conflict-resolution protocol, communication channels).

**Step 5 — Execution with Reflection**
Apply **PDCA cycle** — Plan, Do, Check, Act — for continuous improvement.

**Step 6 — Closure & Handover**
Conduct **retrospective**, document lessons, recognise contributions.

## 3.4 The 5 Dysfunctions of a Team (Patrick Lencioni, 2002) — Pyramidal Derivation

A *top-down hierarchical* framework showing how team dysfunction compounds:

$$
\text{Absence of Trust} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Fear of Conflict} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Lack of Commitment} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Avoidance of Accountability} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Inattention to Results}
$$

- **Level 1 — Absence of Trust:** Team members cannot be vulnerable; they hide weaknesses.
- **Level 2 — Fear of Conflict:** Artificial harmony replaces productive debate.
- **Level 3 — Lack of Commitment:** Buy-in is shallow because members didn’t shape the decision.
- **Level 4 — Avoidance of Accountability:** Low standards because no one calls out mediocrity.
- **Level 5 — Inattention to Results:** Focus shifts to individual status over collective outcomes.

> [!TIP]
> **Reverse Logic for Solutions:** Cure *Level 5* by *starting* at Level 1 — build vulnerability-based trust through personal sharing, personality inventories (MBTI, Belbin), and 1-on-1 check-ins.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**Define the term “Functioning Effectively as an Individual and in a Team”. List any TWO competencies required for each dimension.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Functioning effectively as an individual and in a team refers to the demonstrated ability to plan, execute, and take ownership of assigned tasks autonomously, while simultaneously collaborating with diverse team members to achieve a shared objective within stipulated constraints of time and quality.

**Individual Competencies (1 Mark — any 2):**
1. Self-awareness and self-management
2. Accountability and ownership
3. Time management and SMART goal setting
4. Initiative and adaptability

**Team Competencies (1 Mark — any 2):**
1. Collaboration and shared leadership
2. Effective communication and active listening
3. Conflict resolution (e.g., Thomas-Kilmann modes)
4. Role clarity and adherence to team norms

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**Explain Tuckman’s Five Stages of Team Development. Identify the stage where productivity is typically LOWEST.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Bruce Tuckman (1965) proposed that teams pass through five sequential stages:

| Stage | Characteristic | Productivity |
|---|---|---|
| **Forming** | Polite, exploratory, role ambiguity | Low |
| **Storming** | Conflict, cliques, frustration | **Lowest** ✓ |
| **Norming** | Trust builds, norms emerge | Rising |
| **Performing** | High autonomy, task-focused | Peak |
| **Adjourning** | Closure, reflection | Variable |

**Answer:** Productivity is typically **LOWEST in the Storming stage** due to interpersonal conflict, competing agendas, and unresolved role ambiguity. Managers must apply **collaborative conflict-resolution** (Thomas-Kilmann) and **SBI feedback** to navigate this stage.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *“A team’s effectiveness is determined not by the brilliance of its members but by the diversity of roles they play.”* Critically analyse this statement using **Belbin’s Team Role Theory**. Discuss any **SIX roles** with their characteristic contributions and weaknesses. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Lencioni’s Five Dysfunctions of a Team** as a hierarchical pyramid. For each dysfunction, suggest **ONE concrete intervention** that a project manager can apply in a KTU semester-project setting. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (a) [7 Marks]

**Belbin’s Team Role Theory (Dr. Meredith Belbin, 1981)** posits that a team’s effectiveness is a function of the **balance of roles** rather than the individual brilliance of any single member.

We analyse **six roles**, grouped into clusters:

| # | Role | Cluster | Contribution | Allowable Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plant** | Thinking | Creative, generates original ideas | May be too absorbed to communicate effectively |
| 2 | **Monitor-Evaluator** | Thinking | Strategic, analytical, judges accurately | Lacks drive and ability to inspire others |
| 3 | **Shaper** | Action | Challenging, dynamic, thrives on pressure | Can provoke others, hurt feelings |
| 4 | **Implementer** | Action | Disciplined, reliable, efficient executor | Slow to respond to new possibilities |
| 5 | **Coordinator** | People | Mature, confident, promotes decision-making | Can be seen as manipulating |
| 6 | **Teamworker** | People | Cooperative, perceptive, listens, averts friction | Indecisive in crunch situations |

**Critical Analysis (2 Marks):**
- A team composed solely of *Plants* would generate ideas but never execute.
- A team composed solely of *Implementers* would execute but never innovate.
- A **balanced team** — with at least one role from each cluster — produces the **synergy** that Belbin originally observed in Henley Management College studies.

**Conclusion:** The statement is therefore *valid*; effectiveness emerges from **role diversity**, not from individual brilliance.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (b) [7 Marks]

**Lencioni’s Five Dysfunctions Pyramid (Patrick Lencioni, 2002)** is a hierarchical model where each dysfunction builds on the one below:

**Layer 1 — Absence of Trust (1 Mark):**
*Symptom:* Team members hide weaknesses and avoid vulnerability.
*Intervention (KTU Context):* Conduct **personality-sharing sessions** (e.g., MBTI, Johari Window) at the start of the semester project. Encourage **1-on-1 weekly check-ins**.

**Layer 2 — Fear of Conflict (1 Mark):**
*Symptom:* Artificial harmony; productive disagreement is suppressed.
*Intervention:* Adopt **Thomas-Kilmann Collaborating** mode. Institute a **“disagree-and-commit”** rule during team meetings.

**Layer 3 — Lack of Commitment (1 Mark):**
*Symptom:* Shallow buy-in; ambiguity in decisions.
*Intervention:* Use the **RACI matrix** to clarify who decides. Document every decision in a **shared decision log**.

**Layer 4 — Avoidance of Accountability (1 Mark):**
*Symptom:* Low standards; no one calls out mediocrity.
*Intervention:* Introduce **peer-review sprints** where team members evaluate each other’s deliverables.

**Layer 5 — Inattention to Results (1 Mark):**
*Symptom:* Individual status overrides collective outcomes.
*Intervention:* Set **team-level OKRs (Objectives and Key Results)** and tie grading or recognition to collective outcomes, not just individual contributions.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The hierarchical structure implies that curing the *top* dysfunction requires first healing the *bottom* one — making **trust-building the single highest-leverage intervention**.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Mode Instrument (TKI)** with a neat 2 × 2 classification. Discuss how each of the **five conflict-handling modes** can be applied in a multi-disciplinary engineering project team. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a detailed note on **Goleman’s Five Components of Emotional Intelligence (EQ)**. Explain, with a project-example, how EQ mediates the transition from *individual work* to *effective teamwork*. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (a) [7 Marks]

The **Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Mode Instrument (1974)** classifies conflict-handling behaviour along two axes:

- **Assertiveness** — degree to which one pursues one’s own concerns
- **Cooperativeness** — degree to which one pursues the other party’s concerns

This produces a **2 × 2 matrix** yielding **five modes**:

| Mode | Assertiveness | Cooperativeness | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Competing** (1 Mark) | High | Low | A deadline is breached; team-lead unilaterally shifts priorities |
| **Collaborating** (1 Mark) | High | High | Two subsystems clash in design; team runs a joint root-cause workshop |
| **Compromising** (1 Mark) | Medium | Medium | Two members split a contested presentation slot |
| **Avoiding** (1 Mark) | Low | Low | A low-impact stylistic debate is sidestepped to focus on the demo |
| **Accommodating** (1 Mark) | Low | High | A senior yields a minor coding preference to a junior to build trust |

**Synthesis (2 Marks):** No single mode is universally best. The most effective team members demonstrate **situational flexibility** — switching between modes based on stakes, time, and relationship capital. In KTU project evaluations, examiners reward **mature judgement** in selecting the appropriate mode.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (b) [7 Marks]

**Daniel Goleman (1995)** identified five components of Emotional Intelligence, organised into **Personal Competence** (self) and **Social Competence** (others):

**Personal Competence (Self-Facing):**
1. **Self-Awareness (1 Mark)** — Recognising one’s emotions as they occur.
2. **Self-Regulation (1 Mark)** — Managing impulses and adapting to change.
3. **Motivation (1 Mark)** — Using inner drivers beyond extrinsic reward.

**Social Competence (Other-Facing):**
4. **Empathy (1 Mark)** — Sensing others’ emotional states.
5. **Social Skill (1 Mark)** — Managing relationships and building networks.

**Project Example (2 Marks):**

> Consider a final-year Capstone project where the *Mechanical sub-team* misses a tolerance deadline, threatening the *Electrical sub-team’s* PCB layout.
>
> - A team member with **high Self-Regulation** resists the urge to blame.
> - **Empathy** allows the Electrical lead to understand the supplier delay faced by Mechanical.
> - **Social Skill** enables a joint recovery plan.
> - The team’s **Motivation** sustains them through the reworked weekend.
> - **Self-Awareness** lets the team-lead acknowledge his own anxiety before communicating to the faculty guide.

**Conclusion:** EQ is therefore the **bridge** that converts individual self-mastery into collective synergy — directly mapping to **PO 9 (Individual and Team Work)** in the KTU 2024 scheme.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before the Exam):**
>
> 1. **One-Sided Answers (–2 to –3 Marks):** Students describe only the *team* dimension and ignore *individual* effectiveness. Always structure your answer with **two clear halves** — Individual first, Team second.
>
> 2. **List-Without-Application (–1 to –2 Marks):** Merely *listing* Tuckman’s stages or Belbin’s roles without explaining *when* and *how* to apply them. KTU examiners reward **application**, not recall.
>
> 3. **No Examples (–1 Mark):** Engineering students who fail to give a **project-based or industry example** lose marks under the “application” Bloom’s level.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Bridge Concept (–1 Mark):** Skipping **Emotional Intelligence** as the link between individual and team dimensions. This is a high-frequency *“expected keyword”* in the 2024 scheme marking scheme.
>
> 5. **Mixing Frameworks Without Synthesis (–1 Mark):** Citing Tuckman *and* Lencioni *and* Belbin in the same paragraph without linking them is a structural flaw. **Always end with a synthesis paragraph** that unifies the frameworks.
